ai_strategy.summary: Cornell University's public AI posture combines published guidance, teaching resources, research centers, AI-relevant programs, and research computing. Public evidence points to distributed governance rather than a verified standalone chief AI officer.

key_provisions: Use appropriate or approved AI tools within institutional data rules
Faculty should set clear course-level AI expectations
Users remain responsible for accuracy, attribution, privacy, and academic integrity
Sensitive data should not be entered into unapproved public tools
ai_policy.summary: Cornell pairs IT security guidance for generative AI tools with teaching guidance that leaves course-level permissions to instructors while emphasizing privacy, attribution, and academic integrity.

bodies.name: AI technology and teaching guidance owners
bodies.scope: Distributed responsibility for AI tool guidance, teaching support, and research coordination.

governance_structure.summary: Cornell University's AI governance is distributed across IT, teaching support, and research units.

initiatives.title: Cornell University AI guidance and acceptable-use resources
initiatives.description: Cornell pairs IT security guidance for generative AI tools with teaching guidance that leaves course-level permissions to instructors while emphasizing privacy, attribution, and academic integrity.
initiatives.significance: Institution-level guidance is the baseline signal for responsible AI adoption.

investment.description: Policy and guidance work; no dollar amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: AI use is permitted within privacy, security, course, and attribution guardrails.

initiatives.title: Cornell University generative AI teaching resources
initiatives.description: Cornell University publishes faculty-facing resources for syllabus language, assignments, assessment, and student expectations around generative AI.
initiatives.significance: Teaching guidance moves AI adoption from conduct enforcement into course design.

investment.description: Faculty support resources; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Faculty set course-level rules and students need clear disclosure and permitted-use expectations.

initiatives.title: Cornell AI Initiative
initiatives.description: Cornell University has AI, machine learning, data science, policy, health, or autonomy research structures that organize faculty and graduate research.
initiatives.significance: Dedicated research structures are a core marker of R1 AI maturity.

investment.description: Research center/institute support; aggregate AI-specific amount not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Cross-school or interdisciplinary AI research ecosystem.

initiatives.title: AI-relevant computer science and data science programs
initiatives.description: Cornell University offers AI-relevant degrees, certificates, bootcamps, or structured learning pathways in AI, machine learning, data science, analytics, or computing.
initiatives.significance: AI credentials connect R1 research capacity to student and employer demand.

initiatives.title: Cornell University research computing for AI workloads
initiatives.description: Cornell Center for Advanced Computing provides research computing and data infrastructure used by AI and data-intensive research groups.
initiatives.significance: AI maturity depends on scalable compute as well as policy and curricula.

investment.description: Research computing infrastructure; AI-specific public amount not disclosed.

initiatives.title: Cornell University responsible and equitable AI access
initiatives.description: Cornell University's public AI resources stress transparent expectations, responsible use, and access-aware teaching or public-interest AI work.
initiatives.significance: Equity evidence distinguishes mature AI adoption from simple tool availability.

investment.description: Access and responsible-use work; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Clear AI expectations reduce unequal access, ambiguous enforcement, and privacy risk.

initiatives.title: CTI AI assignment design and GenAI education guidance
initiatives.description: Cornell CTI publishes core principles for GenAI in education plus assignment-design guidance that asks faculty to revisit what they want to assess and how AI changes the evidence of learning.
initiatives.significance: Cornell documents assessment redesign at the instructional-design level, with principles explicitly tied to student learning and faculty judgment.

computing_infrastructure.summary: Cornell CAC's Red Cloud provides on-premises dedicated virtual servers and storage, including NVIDIA T4 and V100 GPU instances for AI/ML workloads, 1.9 PB of raw Ceph storage, and a free exploratory-project allocation for Cornell students, faculty, and staff. CAC impact materials also report millions of Red Cloud compute hours, but total physical GPU inventory was not public.

investments_and_gifts.purpose: AI research, education, or infrastructure capacity.

industry_and_research_partnerships.purpose: Interdisciplinary AI research and education coordination.

student_facing_ai.name: Course-level AI expectations
student_facing_ai.scope: Students follow instructor rules and institutional guidance for AI use.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Responsible and transparent AI use
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Public guidance emphasizes clarity, responsible use, and access-aware teaching or research practices.

regulatory_context.summary: Cornell University operates under federal education, privacy, and research obligations plus campus rules. This sweep found institutional AI guidance but no separate campus-specific AI statute.

narrative_summary: Cornell University is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is visible in responsible-use guidance, classroom resources, AI-relevant research structures, credentials or workforce programming, and research computing. The campus permits AI experimentation with guardrails rather than presenting a simple prohibition. Faculty-facing materials make course policy and assessment design central to implementation, while technology guidance emphasizes privacy, security, attribution, and responsible use. Research evidence comes from AI, machine-learning, data-science, policy, health, autonomy, or computing organizations, supported by high-performance or research computing resources. The profile is strongest on public policy resources, research centers, programs, and infrastructure, and weaker on exact budgets, enterprise license counts, adoption metrics, and named executive ownership. Overall, the institution appears to be in a growing-to-operational phase: AI work is real and distributed across functions, but public evidence does not yet show a single centralized AI operating model with disclosed outcomes.

gaps_acknowledged: Current president, provost, CIO, and chief AI officer were not independently verified in this worker sweep, so leadership names are left null.
Exact enterprise AI contract values, user counts, and adoption metrics were not publicly confirmed.
AI-specific total budget was not disclosed in the public sources used.
No institution-specific AI litigation or major controversy was confirmed.
Cornell discloses Red Cloud GPU instance types and some compute-hour impact reporting, but not total GPU inventory or enterprise AI active-user counts.
